text: Could you expand on who you are looking to collaborate with? Other organizations, individuals, etc.?

elements.text: Could you expand on who you are looking to collaborate with? Other organizations, individuals, etc.?

text: Good question <@U0378MDUG1Y>. Both, but in different ways. In one scenario, we need to be dealing with an organisation – even if it's a small one – and just a front for an individual. But this organisation needs to be eligible so you'll need to check the funding website linked from the doc linked above. If there's no organisation then we can contract an individual to Kendraio but then we still need a partner organisation in the US or Canada. But happy to talk about contracting as a parallel potential.

elements.text: Good question

. Both, but in different ways. In one scenario, we need to be dealing with an organisation – even if it's a small one – and just a front for an individual. But this organisation needs to be eligible so you'll need to check the funding website linked from the doc linked above. If there's no organisation then we can contract an individual to Kendraio but then we still need a partner organisation in the US or Canada. But happy to talk about contracting as a parallel potential.

text:
I am really trying to get AI to do software engineering and be genuinely helpful at the higher end of coding. Still working on it :slightly_smiling_face: but the Observable toolchain I co-produced with AI is a step up from where I was a year ago. The technique that helped was feeding the test suite back into the LLM context so we would co-evolve the suite and implementation at the same time. Furthermore I had extra context tools, so if I wanted it to concentrate on a specific test case I had a UI to select that case. I don't think it could make the whole thing alone yet, I definitely wrote a good chunk of the Observable decompiler/compiler pair, but it was very helpful at
1. Writing semanticallt broken but executing and close first draft of code
2. Choosing which bug to fix next and coming up with a suggestion
3. Using new libraries idiomatically (e.g. acorn-walk)
<https://observablehq.com/@tomlarkworthy/observablejs-toolchain>
I've been wanting a decompiler for observable for ages, and I don't think I could have done it alone either.

text: Really great talk! Sad not to make it in-person but am enjoying catching up with the talks and hope this kind of discussion can carry on in the live programming conference in barcelona

text: <@UBKNXPBAB>'s great LIVE talk on feedback reminded me of Chris Nash's PhD work on feedback and flow in notational use. The diagrams from page 101 are a good place to start <https://www.repository.cam.ac.uk/bitstreams/8c3a332c-74a2-473d-aee2-8bd2f505cf34/download>

elements.text: 's great LIVE talk on feedback reminded me of Chris Nash's PhD work on feedback and flow in notational use. The diagrams from page 101 are a good place to start

text:
yes I think the plural is great! Disassociates it from fascist takes on the future (might sound melodramatic, but is something I often feel around tech 'disruption').
Personally though I think focus on futures is still problematic, when the 'future of programming' really needs to embrace the past. As I said before, I think the 'future of programming' should be read for its irony, as in Bret Victor's talk <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8pTEmbeENF4>
But I guess nuances are for blurbs rather than titles..

text: Computer programs are complex systems so it is impossible to understand them just by reading them.

elements.text: Computer programs are complex systems so it is impossible to understand them just by reading them.

text: For example `(x ^ y) % 9 == 0` is easy to understand as code but when you run it you get something in a completely different domain with effects and relationships that you couldn't have predicted

elements.text: For example
is easy to understand as code but when you run it you get something in a completely different domain with effects and relationships that you couldn't have predicted
